title: Intonation - Pitch & Melody
description: Learn Finnish intonation patterns - falling vs. continuation pitch and how they affect meaning
instructions: Master the pitch patterns and melodies in Finnish speech

structure.description: Finnish intonation has two main patterns: falling pitch (terminal) signals the end of a turn, while flat or rising pitch (continuation) signals the speaker has more to say. Unlike English, Finnish grammar (not pitch) marks questions.
structure.terminal_intonation: Pitch drops at the END of a phrase. Signals: 'I'm finished, your turn to speak.'
structure.continuation_intonation: Pitch stays flat or rises slightly. Signals: 'I have more to say, wait for me.'
structure.questions_marked_by: Grammar endings (-ko/-kö suffix) and question words (mikä, missä, milloin, kuka) – NOT pitch!
key_points: Practice with longer phrases, not single words

questions.text: What does falling intonation at the end of a sentence signal?
options: That you're finished speaking and it's the listener's turn to respond
That the listener should ask for clarification
That you want to continue speaking immediately
questions.explanation: Terminal intonation (falling pitch) signals completion—the speaker is finished and it's the listener's turn.

questions.text: What does continuation intonation (flat or rising) tell the listener?
options: The speaker still has more to say and hasn't finished yet
The sentence is incomplete and grammatically wrong
questions.explanation: Continuation intonation keeps the pitch flat or slightly rising, signaling the speaker isn't finished and has more to say.

questions.text: How do Finnish speakers typically mark YES/NO questions?
options: By raising the pitch significantly at the end
By using the -ko/-kö suffix on the main word, not by pitch
By repeating the last word twice
questions.explanation: Finnish uses grammar (the -ko/-kö suffix) to mark questions, NOT pitch changes.

questions.text: Write the Finnish question 'Should we go for coffee?' and explain what marks it as a question.
questions.explanation: 'Mennäänkö kahville?' The -ko suffix marks it as a question. The pitch can be falling or rising—the grammar does the work.

questions.text: Listen to this sentence. Is the pitch falling (terminal) or rising (continuation)?
questions.explanation: 'Mennään vaan' (Let's go) ends with falling intonation, signaling the speaker is finished.

questions.text: Listen to this story beginning. What intonation pattern does it use?
questions.explanation: The story beginning uses continuation intonation (flat/slightly rising) because the speaker hasn't finished the story yet.

questions.text: Listen to this exchange. Identify what makes the question clear.
questions.explanation: The question is marked by grammar (-ko/-kö), not by rising pitch. Even though both sentences fall, the grammar marks which is the question.

questions.text: Name two situations where you use falling intonation in Finnish.
questions.explanation: Terminal intonation marks the end of a thought or turn in conversation.

questions.text: Describe when you would use continuation intonation in Finnish.
questions.explanation: Use continuation intonation when you're in the middle of speaking and have more to say.

questions.text: When practicing Finnish intonation, what should you focus on?
options: Repeating and comparing longer phrases to native speakers
questions.explanation: The best way to practice intonation is to repeat longer phrases and compare your pitch curves to native speakers. Single words won't help!
